title: AI coding tools loud in the feed, absent from the job spec
thesis: AI coding tools own the developer conversation but are almost entirely absent from the job spec — the tools everyone is talking about (claude-code, codex, cursor) are the ones nobody is hiring for, while the "boring" stack (Python, Kubernetes, React) still runs hiring. The divergence is between what developers discuss and what employers ask for.
period: Conversation window Feb–Jun 2026 (complete months, HN feeds + Lobsters). Job-mention window is the entity_stats job_mentions_7d series, 5 weekly snapshots 2026-06-12 -> 2026-07-06; headline job figures use the latest snapshot (2026-07-06). Heavy caveat below.
findings:
1. The flagship stat — the 4th most-discussed technology on HN/Lobsters had one job posting. In June 2026, claude-code drew 254 distinct conversation stories, ranking #4 of all 65 tracked entities (tied with Rust, ahead of Python's 144). In the latest weekly job snapshot it appears in exactly 1 posting. Python — which claude-code out-talked in conversation — appears in 30.
   Leaderboard SQL: WITH conv AS (SELECT DISTINCT url_hash, entity FROM raw_items, jsonb_array_elements_text(entities) AS entity WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-06-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-01') SELECT entity, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS june_stories FROM conv GROUP BY entity ORDER BY june_stories DESC LIMIT 15
   Returned (top): claude 640, anthropic 296, rust 254, claude-code 254, openai 208, mcp 198, gpt 184, python 144, codex 133, postgres 97, cursor 83, github-copilot 65.

2. The conversation-per-job-mention ratio quantifies the gap. Joining June conversation stories to the latest job snapshot, agentic AI coding tools sit at 80–254+ conversation stories per job mention (or infinite, at 0 jobs); established tech sits at 1–7.
   SQL: WITH conv AS (SELECT entity, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS conv FROM raw_items, jsonb_array_elements_text(entities) AS entity WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-06-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-01' AND entity IN ('cursor','claude-code','codex','windsurf','zed','aider','cline','github-copilot','python','kubernetes','react','docker','typescript','golang','postgres') GROUP BY entity), jobs AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug AS entity, value AS jobs FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='job_mentions_7d' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at DESC) SELECT c.entity, c.conv AS june_conv_stories, j.jobs AS job_mentions_latest, round(c.conv::numeric / NULLIF(j.jobs,0),1) AS conv_per_job FROM conv c JOIN jobs j USING(entity) ORDER BY c.conv DESC
   Returned: claude-code 254 conv / 1 job (254:1); python 144 / 30 (4.8:1); codex 133 / 0 (inf); postgres 97 / 3 (32.3:1); cursor 83 / 1 (83:1); github-copilot 65 / 5 (13:1); typescript 43 / 7 (6.1:1); react 40 / 7 (5.7:1); kubernetes 29 / 10 (2.9:1); docker 29 / 4 (7.3:1); golang 6 / 6 (1.0:1); windsurf 2 / 0; cline 2 / 0; aider 1 / 1.

3. Cohort totals: the AI-coding cohort out-talks the boring stack yet is invisible in hiring. Summing the table above — agentic AI coding tools (claude-code, codex, cursor, windsurf, zed, aider, cline; excludes github-copilot) = 475 June conversation stories but 3 job mentions -> 158 stories per job. The established stack (python, kubernetes, react, docker, typescript, golang) = 291 conversation stories and 64 job mentions -> 4.5 stories per job. So the chatter-to-hiring ratio is ~35x higher for the new AI tools. Of the ~120 total job mentions in the latest weekly snapshot across all 65 entities, the entire agentic-AI-tool cohort accounts for 3 (~2.5%); Python alone accounts for 30 (25%). (Derived by summing finding-2 rows; the 120 total is from finding 5.)

4. It's a consistent structural gap, not one noisy week. Across all 5 weekly snapshots the agentic tools stay pinned near zero in job mentions: cursor 3->1->0->1->1, codex 5->3->0->0->0, windsurf/zed/cline/jetbrains-ai flat at 0 throughout, claude-code decaying 6->3->1->1->1. github-copilot is the lone exception (1->5->6->4->5) — the mature, brand-name product that predates the agentic wave and has crossed into hiring language.
   SQL: SELECT entity_slug, date_trunc('day',captured_at)::date AS day, value FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='job_mentions_7d' AND entity_slug IN ('cursor','claude-code','codex','windsurf','zed','aider','github-copilot','cline','jetbrains-ai') ORDER BY entity_slug, day
   Returned the series above.

5. Job data caveat (must be in the piece). job_mentions_7d is a rolling 7-day count captured weekly over only ~4 weeks; absolute volumes are small (~103–125 mentions/week across all 65 entities), so single-tool figures are noisy. The first snapshot (2026-06-12) is an anomalous outlier (353 total, entities appear double-captured) and should be excluded from any trend.
   SQL: SELECT date_trunc('day',captured_at) AS day, count(DISTINCT entity_slug) AS entities, sum(value) AS total_mentions FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='job_mentions_7d' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
   Returned: 2026-06-12 -> 353; 2026-06-15 -> 124; 2026-06-22 -> 103; 2026-06-29 -> 125; 2026-07-06 -> 120. Measurement floor: a job ad saying "experience with AI coding assistants a plus" wouldn't tag a specific tool slug — so the job side undercounts intent, but that reinforces the thesis.
context: Deliberately distinct from published "AI Coding Wars" (which pitted the tools against each other). Axis here is conversation-vs-hiring, not tool-vs-tool. Can nod to that piece and to "The Argument About MCP Is Ending; Its Adoption Is Not" (same lag pattern: adoption ahead of formal recognition). No open bets graded here.
candidate_bets:
- By 2026-12-31, at least one agentic AI coding tool (cursor, claude-code, or codex) will exceed 5 mentions in a single weekly job_mentions_7d snapshot (currently max 1 for cursor/claude-code, 0 for codex). Why: hiring language lags tool adoption 6–18 months. Confidence medium. 2026-12-31.
- github-copilot will remain the most-hired AI coding tool (highest job_mentions_7d) of the cohort through 2026-09-30. Why: brand maturity, enterprise procurement inertia. Confidence high. 2026-09-30.
cover_idea: A crowded, noisy developer conference hall — a huge glowing banner "CLAUDE CODE / CURSOR / CODEX" over a cheering crowd — while just outside, a lone recruiter holds a small "Now Hiring" sign listing only "Python · Kubernetes · React," with tumbleweed. The loudest room in tech, the quietest job board. Alt: two speech bubbles over a developer — a giant one full of AI-tool logos, a tiny résumé/job-ad one still saying "Python."

title: The great unbundling of the agent stack
thesis: The agent-orchestration frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI, LlamaIndex) are being quietly abandoned — their developer conversation has collapsed to a rounding error and their weekly installs are in absolute decline — while the raw model SDKs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini) hold flat at a base 20-40x larger; developers are dropping the framework layer, not the models.
period:
Conversation Feb–Jun 2026 (July partial, caveated). Install/stars snapshot Jun 12 – Jul 8 2026 (~4 weeks; a snapshot, not a trend — caveat).

IMPORTANT METHODOLOGY CORRECTION (retracting half the seed): The seed's PyPI "surge" numbers (openai +22.5%, gemini +25.6%, langchain +15.1%, etc.) are contaminated by a data artifact. Every one of the six packages jumps ~18–25% on the exact same day, Jun 18, then plateaus — a uniform step change across unrelated packages is a scraper/methodology change (likely mirror inclusion), not organic growth. Naively comparing Jun 12 -> Jul 8 bakes this step into every number. All install figures below use the clean post-step window (Jun 18 -> last reading). Do not repeat the seed's raw growth percentages.
findings:
1. Within the clean window, the three frameworks are ALL declining on PyPI; the three SDKs are flat at a massive base. Core divergence, and it survives the artifact because measured entirely post-step.
   - Frameworks: langchain 78.94M -> 74.18M/wk = -6.0%; crewai 2.90M -> 2.22M/wk = -23.2%; llamaindex 1.90M -> 1.60M/wk = -16.0%. (crewai and llamaindex both end below their pre-step Jun 12 levels of 2.30M and 1.66M — decline the artifact can't explain away.)
   - SDKs: openai 87.31M -> 86.67M = -0.7% (flat); anthropic 37.03M -> 37.07M = +0.1% (flat); gemini 65.56M -> 68.07M = +3.8% (last reading Jul 3, already off its Jun 29 peak of 70.4M).
   - SQL: SELECT entity_slug, captured_at::date AS d, value FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='pypi_downloads_weekly' AND entity_slug IN ('openai','anthropic','gemini','langchain','crewai','llamaindex') ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at;
   - Returned: post-Jun 18 endpoints: openai Jun18=87,310,411 / Jul6=86,667,935; anthropic 37,026,718 / 37,072,172; gemini 65,564,423 / Jul3=68,070,999; langchain 78,939,972 / 74,177,433; crewai Jun19=2,897,146 / Jul8=2,224,020; llamaindex 1,901,694 / 1,597,972. The uniform Jun 17->18 jump (anthropic 30.8M->37.0M, langchain 65.8M->78.9M, gemini 54.5M->65.6M, openai 73.2M->87.3M) is the artifact.

2. Scale gap: the entire "orchestration framework" category is dwarfed by any single model SDK. CrewAI (2.2M/wk) and LlamaIndex (1.6M/wk) each run at ~2-3% of OpenAI's install volume (86.7M/wk). Only LangChain (74M/wk) is SDK-scale — and it's the one clearly declining. (Same query/data as Finding 1.)

3. Developer conversation on the frameworks has collapsed to a fraction of a percent — and halved for the only one that ever registered. LangChain distinct HN+Lobsters stories: Feb 14, Mar 15, Apr 11, May 12, Jun 6 — more than halved off the Feb–Mar plateau. As share of all tagged conversation stories that month, ~0.47% (Feb) -> 0.23% (Jun). CrewAI: 3 -> 4 -> 0 -> 0 -> 1 (essentially gone after March). LlamaIndex: never exceeded 1 story/month. July partial (8 days) so its uptick to 6 is noise — caveat.
   - SQL (per-entity, independent tagging): SELECT ent, mo, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS stories FROM (SELECT url_hash, to_char(source_created_at,'YYYY-MM') AS mo, e.ent FROM raw_items, unnest(ARRAY['langchain','crewai','llamaindex','openai','anthropic','gemini']) AS e(ent) WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-02-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-09' AND jsonb_exists(entities, e.ent)) t GROUP BY ent, mo ORDER BY ent, mo;
   - Returned: langchain 14/15/11/12/6/6 (Feb–Jul); crewai 3/4/-/-/1/-; llamaindex -/-/1/1/1/-. Model-provider contrast (brand mentions not SDK-specific): openai 208/225/254/229/208/45; anthropic 233/247/284/194/296/65; gemini 108/113/81/99/50/13.
   - SoV denominator SQL: SELECT to_char(source_created_at,'YYYY-MM') AS mo, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS tagged_stories FROM raw_items WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-02-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-09' AND jsonb_array_length(entities) > 0 GROUP BY mo ORDER BY mo;
   - Returned: Feb 2965, Mar 3296, Apr 2917, May 2490, Jun 2583, Jul 648 (partial).

4. GitHub stars keep rising for all three frameworks — the lagging vanity metric that masks the decline. crewai +3.3% (53,347 -> 55,115), langchain +1.5% (139,124 -> 141,249), llamaindex +1.2% (50,097 -> 50,719) over the same ~4 weeks. Stars are cumulative and only go up; crewai adds stars fastest even as installs fall 23% and conversation is gone. Rhetorical device: the star count says "healthy," the install curve and silence say otherwise.
   - SQL: SELECT entity_slug, metric, min(value) FILTER (WHERE captured_at::date <= '2026-06-14') AS early, max(value) FILTER (WHERE captured_at::date >= '2026-07-05') AS late FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='github_stars' AND entity_slug IN ('openai','anthropic','gemini','langchain','crewai','llamaindex') GROUP BY entity_slug, metric ORDER BY entity_slug;
   - Returned: anthropic 3617->3717, crewai 53347->55115, langchain 139124->141249, llamaindex 50097->50719, openai 30981->31109 (gemini has no stars series).

HONEST CAVEATS: Do NOT claim SDKs are "surging" on installs — within the clean window they are flat; the story is divergence (frameworks fall, SDKs hold), not SDK acceleration. The openai/anthropic/gemini conversation tags capture all mention of the company/model, not the Python SDK — lean on installs for the SDK side, conversation for the framework side. "langchain" is a single PyPI series here; can't decompose metapackage vs core vs community. 4-week install/star window is a snapshot; conversation trend (Feb–Jun) is more robust; July partial.
context: Adjacent to published "The Argument About MCP Is Ending; Its Adoption Is Not" — same theme of an abstraction layer's conversation cooling. This is the mirror image: the abstraction layer (orchestration frameworks) is cooling in conversation AND losing installs, i.e. being unbundled rather than quietly adopted. Distinct from AI-coding tools (library/framework layer, not IDE tools). No open bet covers frameworks.
candidate_bets:
- CrewAI weekly PyPI installs stay below 2.5M through 2026-09-30 (last reading 2.22M, -23% in-window). Confidence medium.
- LangChain's HN+Lobsters SoV stays <= 0.35%/month for a full non-partial month before 2026-09-30 (Jun already 0.23%). Confidence medium.
- LlamaIndex records <= 2 HN+Lobsters stories in any single month through 2026-09-30 (never exceeded 1). Confidence high.
cover_idea: A shipping container being unpacked / unbundled on a dock — the outer crate stencilled "FRAMEWORK" pried open and set aside, while three sealed pristine inner boxes stencilled with plain maker's marks (the model SDKs) get loaded onto the truck. The empty framework crate left on the quay, still bearing a bright "* 141k" sticker that no longer means anything.

title: vLLM, the production-inference dark horse
thesis: vLLM is quietly winning the self-hosted LLM inference race — its GitHub growth, developer conversation, and engagement quality are all climbing while Ollama, the hobbyist default, plateaus, signalling that self-hosting is maturing from tinkering into production serving.
period: Conversation Feb–Jul 2026 (July partial, 8 days); GitHub/registry metrics 2026-06-12 -> 2026-07-08 (~4-week snapshot — caveat: short window).
context: Adjacent to but distinct from published "AI Coding Wars" (that was the authoring layer; this is the serving/inference layer). No prior issue covered self-hosted inference or the vLLM/Ollama split. Same theme as "The Argument About MCP Is Ending; Its Adoption Is Not" — a tool maturing from debate into quiet production adoption. No open bet touches inference infra.
candidate_bets:
- vLLM's share of the vLLM+Ollama HN/lobsters conversation stays >= 45% (its June level) through the next full month. Why: June crossover + partial-July 75% suggest a durable shift. Confidence medium. Resolve 2026-08-31.
- vLLM continues to out-add Ollama in GitHub stars by percentage over the next snapshot window. Why: 3.6x gap is wide and consistent with a maturing-adoption curve. Confidence medium-high. Resolve 2026-08-15.
cover_idea: A quiet, understated workhorse (a plain grey draft horse, or a plain industrial server rack) in the foreground actually pulling the load, while a brightly-painted show pony / hobbyist toy llama gets the spotlight and applause behind it — tortoise-beats-hare "quiet winner" framing. Muted serious palette for the workhorse; loud but hollow colour for the crowd-pleaser.

title: Chinese open-weight models rising in Western dev conversation
thesis: In Hacker News and Lobsters conversation, the AI-model "challenger tier" is realigning — DeepSeek (Chinese, open-weight) has roughly doubled its share of voice and now draws the highest engagement of any non-frontier model, converging with a fading Gemini; the open-weight center of gravity is tilting east, though the move is led by DeepSeek specifically rather than all three Chinese labs.
period: Feb 1 – Jun 30 2026 (five complete months; July excluded as partial). Conversation sources only: hn_top/new/best/show/ask + lobsters.
findings:
1. Share of voice WITHIN the ai_model category (10 slugs: anthropic, claude, deepseek, gemini, gpt, kimi, llama, mistral, openai, qwen). Denominator = sum of per-model distinct-story counts that month (standard SoV; multi-model stories count toward each model). Monthly totals: Feb 1617, Mar 1837, Apr 1826, May 1379, Jun 1479.
   - DeepSeek: 1.7% -> 0.5% -> 2.0% -> 4.1% -> 3.2% (Feb->Jun). Roughly doubled; raw stories 28->47.
   - Qwen: 0.4% -> 1.2% -> 0.9% -> 0.9% -> 1.2%. Up ~3x but tiny/noisy base (6->18 stories) — FRAGILE, do not headline alone.
   - Kimi: 0.6% -> 0.7% -> 0.9% -> 0.7% -> 0.5%. FLAT-TO-DOWN — does NOT support the rise; do not lump it in with DeepSeek/Qwen.
   - Gemini: 6.7% -> 6.2% -> 4.4% -> 7.2% -> 3.4%. Clear fade with a May spike; June is a sharp drop (raw 108->50).
   - Mistral: 0.7% -> 1.0% -> 0.6% -> 1.5% -> 0.6%. No clean fade — noisy on a small base (12->9). The "Mistral fades" leg is WEAK; the honest fade story is Gemini, not Mistral.
   - Llama (Meta, Western open-weight): 0.6% -> 1.0% -> 1.0% -> 1.7% -> 1.3%. Also rose (raw 10->19). "open-weight rising" is broader than China — caveat "shifting east".
   - Crossover: DeepSeek was ~1/4 of Gemini's volume in Feb (28 vs 108) and nearly matched it by June (47 vs 50).

   SQL:
   WITH conv AS (
     SELECT url_hash, date_trunc('month', source_created_at)::date AS mo, e AS slug
     FROM raw_items, jsonb_array_elements_text(entities) e
     WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters')
       AND source_created_at >= '2026-02-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-01'
       AND e IN ('anthropic','claude','deepseek','gemini','gpt','kimi','llama','mistral','openai','qwen')
   )
   SELECT mo, slug, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS stories
   FROM conv GROUP BY mo, slug ORDER BY mo, slug
   RETURNED (raw distinct stories/mo Feb->Jun): DeepSeek 28/10/37/56/47; Qwen 6/22/16/12/18; Kimi 10/13/17/9/8; Gemini 108/113/81/99/50; Mistral 12/18/11/21/9; Llama 10/18/19/23/19. Frontier context: claude ~785/973/899/594/640, gpt ~217/198/208/142/184, openai ~208/225/254/229/208, anthropic ~233/247/284/194/296 — giants dwarf every challenger; this is a challenger-tier story.

2. Engagement — DeepSeek stories draw the highest HN engagement of the entire challenger tier. Feb–Jun HN stories (each url_hash to one slug alphabetically — see caveat): DeepSeek avg peak_score 45 (max 2036), avg comments 24 (max 1551); Mistral avg 30; Qwen avg 27; Kimi avg 20; Gemini avg 18; Llama avg 7. DeepSeek avg score ~2.5x Gemini's and owns the single biggest hit (2036 pts). Medians all ~2–3 (HN dead-post tail), so averages driven by genuine blockbusters.
   CAVEAT: alphabetical single-slug assignment credits a deepseek+gemini story to deepseek — directional not exact.

   SQL:
   WITH s AS (
     SELECT DISTINCT ON (url_hash) url_hash, peak_score, peak_comments, e AS slug
     FROM raw_items, jsonb_array_elements_text(entities) e
     WHERE source_slug LIKE 'hn_%'
       AND source_created_at >= '2026-02-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-01'
       AND e IN ('deepseek','qwen','kimi','gemini','mistral','llama')
     ORDER BY url_hash, e
   )
   SELECT slug, count(*) stories, round(avg(peak_score)) avg_score, max(peak_score) max_score,
     round(avg(peak_comments)) avg_comments, max(peak_comments) max_comments,
     percentile_cont(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY peak_score)::int median_score
   FROM s GROUP BY slug ORDER BY avg_score DESC
   RETURNED: deepseek {178 stories, avg 45, max 2036, avg_comments 24, max_comments 1551, median 3}; mistral {68, avg 30, max 720, median 2}; qwen {70, avg 27, max 777, median 2}; kimi {52, avg 20, max 448, median 2}; gemini {447, avg 18, max 952, median 2}; llama {87, avg 7, median 2}.

WHAT HOLDS / WHAT DOESN'T:
- HOLDS: DeepSeek's share roughly doubled Feb->Jun; highest engagement in tier; DeepSeek/Gemini converged from ~4:1 to near-parity.
- FRAGILE: Qwen (tiny base — corroborates, doesn't stand alone).
- DOES NOT HOLD: "Kimi rising" (flat-to-down); "Mistral fading" (noisy). The clean fade is Gemini.
- QUALIFIER on "shifting east": Llama (Western open-weight) also rose — deepest true story is "open-weight gaining on proprietary challengers, DeepSeek leads."

title: Astro's quiet frontend breakout
thesis: Astro is the quiet outlier of the frontend framework field — it is the only one whose npm installs and GitHub stars are still climbing while every incumbent (React, Vue, Angular, Svelte) flatlines or shrinks, yet it draws almost no developer conversation. But the headline "+30% surge" is a data artifact; the real, defensible gap is a steady ~9% install rise and star growth running 4-5x its rivals, against near-silence in the discourse. Adoption is running modestly ahead of attention, not exploding.
period: 2026-06-14 -> 2026-07-08 for registry/stars (the clean capture window; see finding 1). Mindshare Jan->Jul 2026. Entire entity_stats history is only ~4 weeks and July is a partial month — a snapshot, not a long-run trend; caveat heavily.
findings:
1. THE SEED "+30%" IS AN ARTIFACT — DO NOT PRINT IT. The 06-12 baseline (astro 2.71M) is stale: the 06-12 and 06-13 values are byte-identical, then on 06-14 astro (+19%), nextjs (+20.5%) and react-native (+21.3%) ALL jump ~20% on the exact same day while react (+1.8%), vue (+3.6%), angular (+2.8%) and svelte (+0.5%) barely move. Three packages stepping up ~20% in unison on one date is a collector regime-change, not organic demand. Measured from the first clean reading (06-14), astro grew from 3,223,333 to 3,514,613 = +9.0%. That is the honest number.
   SQL: SELECT entity_slug, captured_at::date AS d, value FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='npm_downloads_weekly' AND entity_slug IN ('astro','react','vue','angular','svelte','nextjs','react-native') ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at
   Returned (astro): 06-12 2,706,642 -> 06-13 2,706,642 (identical) -> 06-14 3,223,333 -> 06-24 3,552,030 -> 06-30 3,549,234 -> 07-04 3,670,750 (peak) -> 07-08 3,514,613. Rose ~10 days then PLATEAUED at ~3.5M. Durable step-up + plateau, not a spike-and-revert, not accelerating.

2. AGAINST A CLEAN BASELINE, ASTRO IS THE ONLY FRONTEND FRAMEWORK STILL GROWING. npm change 06-14->07-08: astro +9.0%, nextjs +2.7%, react +0.5%, react-native -0.4%, vue -3.9%, svelte -4.3%, angular -9.6%. Astro's install growth is ~3x the next-best (nextjs); incumbents are net flat-to-shrinking.
   SQL: same series as finding 1, deltas from 06-14 and 07-08 rows.
   Returned: astro 3,223,333->3,514,613; nextjs 37,366,740->38,376,400; react 140,918,490->141,560,512; react-native 9,602,220->9,559,897; vue 13,334,199->12,809,227; svelte 4,814,368->4,606,800; angular 6,062,225->5,481,412.

3. THIS IS AN ASTRO STORY, NOT A META-FRAMEWORK STORY. The seed flagged nextjs +24% and react-native +21% as fellow surgers, but both are pure 06-14 artifact: cleaned, nextjs is only +2.7% and react-native is -0.4%. Astro's +9% stands alone. (react-native's apparent surge also overlaps the already-published mobile story — steer clear.)

4. GITHUB STARS CORROBORATE INDEPENDENTLY — ASTRO ACCRETES INTEREST 4-5x FASTER THAN PEERS. Stars 06-14->07-08: astro 60,134->60,782 = +648 (+1.08%); nextjs +380 (+0.27%); svelte +227 (+0.26%); vue +105 (+0.20%); react +456 (+0.19%); angular +156 (+0.16%). Astro's percentage star growth is the highest in the cohort by a wide margin, untouched by the npm artifact.
   SQL: SELECT entity_slug, metric, min(value) FILTER (WHERE captured_at::date='2026-06-14') AS jun14, max(value) FILTER (WHERE captured_at::date='2026-07-08') AS jul8 FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='github_stars' AND entity_slug IN ('astro','react','vue','angular','svelte','nextjs') GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY 1
   Returned: astro 60134/60782; angular 100329/100485; nextjs 139986/140366; react 245821/246277; svelte 87221/87448; vue 53824/53929.

5. THE DISCOURSE IGNORES IT — THIS IS THE DIVERGENCE. Distinct HN+lobsters stories/month: astro is a rounding error — Jan 2, Feb 4, Mar 11 (its peak, likely a release), Apr 5, May 6, Jun 4, Jul 1 (partial). React runs 40-66/mo, nextjs 16-30/mo. Within the 6-framework cohort, astro's share of voice was ~5.6% in June (4 of 72 tagged stories) vs react's ~56% (40 of 72), and astro's conversation is flat-to-declining even as installs/stars climb.
   SQL: SELECT e AS entity, to_char(date_trunc('month',source_created_at),'YYYY-MM') AS mo, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS stories FROM raw_items, jsonb_array_elements_text(entities) AS e WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND e IN ('astro','react','vue','angular','svelte','nextjs') AND source_created_at >= '2026-01-01' GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY 2,1
   Returned (June 2026): angular 3, astro 4, nextjs 16, react 40, svelte 6, vue 3. (March: astro 11, react 66, nextjs 25, svelte 10, vue 5, angular 1 = 118 total, astro SoV 9.3%.)
context: Distinct from the four published stories — frontend framework divergence, not AI-coding/mobile/supply-chain/MCP. react-native's fake "surge" intersects the retired mobile story; keep it out. No open bet covers Astro. Methodological sibling to the MCP "adoption != conversation" story but a different ecosystem.
candidate_bets:
- Astro npm weekly downloads stay above 3.4M (its clean-window floor) through 2026-09-30. Why: ~3.5M level held 3+ weeks after the mid-June step-up. Confidence medium (only ~4 weeks clean history). 2026-09-30.
- Astro's HN+lobsters SoV within the frontend-framework cohort stays below 12% through 2026-09-30 (conversation does NOT catch up to adoption). Why: SoV range-bound 5-9% all year. Confidence medium-high. 2026-09-30.
cover_idea:
A packed, noisy football stadium of fans all facing and cheering one floodlit player (React) at midfield — while in an unlit corner of the same pitch, a lone player in an Astro shirt quietly stacks the scoreboard higher, unwatched. The crowd's gaze diverging from where the score is actually moving.

WRITER CAVEAT: the "+30%" figure from the raw pull is a collection artifact and must not be published; defensible claims are +9% installs, +1.1% stars (4-5x peers), and near-zero conversation, all over a short ~4-week snapshot.

title: Database consolidation — MySQL, the silent incumbent
thesis: The developer database conversation has collapsed onto two names — Postgres (a clear majority of all database talk, every single month) and SQLite (a durable, distant second) — while every other engine fights over scraps; the most striking case is MySQL, which has faded to single-digit mindshare in the conversation yet still pulls as many job postings as Postgres. MySQL is not dead, it's the silent incumbent: nobody discusses it, everybody still runs it.
period:
Feb 1 – Jul 8 2026 (conversation bucketed by post month on HN + Lobsters; July is PARTIAL — only ~8 days — caveat). Registry/GitHub/jobs signals are a ~4-week snapshot only (2026-06-12 -> 2026-07-08) — caveat.

CORRECTION TO THE ORIGINAL LANE: The "Postgres + its managed ecosystem (Supabase, Neon)" framing does NOT hold in the conversation data. Supabase sits at 6–8% and Neon is effectively 0% of database voice. The real #2 is SQLite, not the managed cloud players. And "MySQL = near-zero hiring" is FALSE — see finding 3. Reframed accordingly; the honest story is stronger.
findings:
1. Postgres owns a majority of the database conversation every month; SQLite is the only other engine with real, sustained voice. Share of database-category voice (distinct stories / total database-tagged mentions that month):
   - Postgres: 54.2% / 50.3% / 55.5% / 48.2% / 53.3% / 56.7% (Feb->Jul)
   - SQLite: 19.4% / 26.9% / 21.9% / 24.1% / 23.6% / 25.0%
   - Together Postgres+SQLite = 73–82% of all database talk every month.
   SQL: WITH m AS (SELECT to_char(source_created_at,'YYYY-MM') AS mo, e.entity_slug, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS stories FROM raw_items, jsonb_array_elements_text(entities) AS e(entity_slug) WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-02-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-08-01' AND e.entity_slug IN ('postgres','mysql','sqlite','redis','supabase','neon','turso') GROUP BY 1,2) SELECT mo, entity_slug, stories, round(100.0*stories/sum(stories) OVER (PARTITION BY mo),1) AS pct FROM m ORDER BY mo, stories DESC;
   Returned (raw stories): Postgres 109/88/86/66/97/34; SQLite 39/47/34/33/43/15; shares above. Postgres never below 48%; SQLite never below 19%.

2. MySQL has faded to single-digit mindshare and its high point was the start of the window. MySQL share of database voice: Feb 10.9% (22) -> Mar 2.3% (4) -> Apr 6.5% (10) -> May 6.6% (9) -> Jun 8.8% (16) -> Jul 3.3% (2, partial). Now interchangeable with Redis (6–13%) and Supabase (6–8%) at the bottom, well behind SQLite, ~6x smaller than Postgres. (Same query/result as finding 1.)

3. THE DIVERGENCE: MySQL is nearly tied with Postgres for TOP hiring signal, even though it barely registers in conversation. job_mentions_7d over the 4-week snapshot (n=6 snapshots per entity — TINY sample, caveat hard): Postgres avg 2.0 (min 1, max 3); MySQL avg 1.5 (min 0, max 7 — the single highest reading of any DB in the window); Redis avg 0.5; SQLite/Supabase/Neon/Turso 0.0 across the board. Latest snapshot (2026-07-06): postgres 3, redis 1, mysql 0, rest 0 — this single day understates MySQL's window average.
   SQL: WITH j AS (SELECT entity_slug, value FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='job_mentions_7d' AND entity_slug IN ('postgres','mysql','sqlite','redis','supabase','neon','turso')) SELECT entity_slug, min(value) mn, max(value) mx, round(avg(value),1) avg_over_window, count(*) n_snapshots FROM j GROUP BY entity_slug ORDER BY avg_over_window DESC;
   Returned: postgres (mn1,mx3,avg2.0); mysql (mn0,mx7,avg1.5); redis (avg0.5); neon/sqlite/supabase/turso all 0.0. The databases the conversation loves most (SQLite, Supabase) generate zero hiring; MySQL — invisible in conversation — is a top-2 hiring signal.

4. The managed-Postgres cloud (Supabase/Neon/Turso) is loud on installs but quiet in conversation and absent from hiring — an adoption/discourse split, not a conversation takeover. Over the ~4-week snapshot: Supabase npm_downloads_weekly +25.3% (17.2M -> 21.5M/wk), GitHub stars +1.7% (104,082 -> 105,873); Neon stars +1.3%; Turso stars +0.6%; Redis stars +0.7%. Yet in conversation Supabase is 6–8%, Neon ~0%, Turso 0–2%, and all three post 0 job mentions.
   SQL: WITH s AS (SELECT entity_slug, metric, value, captured_at, first_value(value) OVER (PARTITION BY entity_slug,metric ORDER BY captured_at) AS first_v, last_value(value) OVER (PARTITION BY entity_slug,metric ORDER BY captured_at ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING) AS last_v FROM entity_stats WHERE metric IN ('github_stars','npm_downloads_weekly') AND entity_slug IN ('postgres','supabase','neon','turso','redis')) SELECT entity_slug, metric, first_v, last_v, CASE WHEN first_v>0 THEN round(100.0*(last_v-first_v)/first_v,1) END AS pct_change FROM s GROUP BY 1,2,3,4 ORDER BY 2,5 DESC NULLS LAST;
   Returned: supabase npm +25.3%; supabase stars +1.7%; neon stars +1.3%; redis stars +0.7%; turso stars +0.6%. (Postgres has no npm/stars row — it isn't a single package; note this so the writer doesn't imply Postgres is stagnating.)

5. Scope note the writer must respect: DevTrends tracks exactly 7 database engines (postgres, mysql, sqlite, redis, supabase, neon, turso). No MongoDB, MariaDB, PlanetScale, CockroachDB, DuckDB, ClickHouse, etc. So "database conversation" means "the relational/embedded/cache set DevTrends watches," not literally every database. Frame claims as share within tracked engines.
   SQL: SELECT entity_slug FROM entity_stats WHERE entity_slug IN ('mongodb','mariadb','planetscale','cockroachdb','duckdb','clickhouse','dynamodb','cassandra','elasticsearch') GROUP BY entity_slug; -> returned [] (none tracked).
context: Sharpest framing: go with "MySQL's silent retirement" / silent-incumbent, NOT "Postgres-industrial-complex." Postgres dominance is real but steady-state (majority every month, no dramatic move) — the backdrop, not the news. The news is the divergence in finding 3. Postgres+SQLite consolidation is the setup; MySQL's conversation-vs-hiring split is the payoff. Distinct from prior coverage (database-engine story). Do NOT overclaim on jobs: 6 snapshots, values 0–7 — supports "MySQL still a top hiring signal / not dead," not precise ratios. If one number: "MySQL averaged 1.5 job mentions vs Postgres's 2.0 over four weeks."
candidate_bets:
- Postgres holds >45% of tracked-database conversation share in every month through 2026-09-30. Confidence high (48–57% for six straight months).
- SQLite remains the #2 tracked database by conversation share (ahead of MySQL, Redis, Supabase) in Aug and Sep 2026. Confidence medium-high.
- MySQL's tracked-database conversation share stays in single digits (<12%) every month through 2026-09-30 while its job_mentions_7d snapshots stay >= Redis's. Confidence medium (jobs sample thin).
cover_idea: A grand, brightly-lit museum hall. Two massive marble pedestals up front, spotlit and crowded — labelled "POSTGRES" and "SQLITE." Off in a dim side alcove, roped off and unvisited, a working industrial engine still humming and turning, plaque reading "MYSQL — still running the building." The celebrated exhibits vs the unglamorous machine that keeps the lights on.

title: n8n, the fastest-growing repo nobody argues about
thesis: n8n is the developer economy's biggest quiet winner — it is adding stars faster than all but two repos (both AI coding assistants already covered), leads every tracked application in absolute Docker-pull growth, and ranks #2 in npm download growth, yet commands essentially zero developer conversation. It is the invisible infrastructure of the agent era: everyone is deploying it, almost nobody is arguing about it.
period: 27-day metrics snapshot 2026-06-12 -> 2026-07-08 (entity_stats history only spans ~4 weeks — caveat as a snapshot, not a long trend). Conversation trend Jan–Jul 2026 (July partial).
findings:
1. n8n ranks #3 of all 45 star-tracked entities in absolute stars gained (+3,392), behind only codex (+5,459) and claude-code (+4,720) — both AI coding assistants already covered in "The AI Coding Wars." Among everything NOT an AI coding tool, n8n is #1. Off the second-largest active base in the set (195k stars), so the gain is a true "large repo still accelerating" story, not a small-base artifact. By percentage its 1.76% is only mid-pack — frame as ABSOLUTE growth on a large base, NOT "fastest-growing by %."
   SQL: WITH b AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value AS v_start FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='github_stars' AND captured_at::date>='2026-06-12' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at ASC), e AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value AS v_end FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='github_stars' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at DESC) SELECT e.entity_slug, b.v_start, e.v_end, (e.v_end-b.v_start) AS abs_delta, round(100.0*(e.v_end-b.v_start)/NULLIF(b.v_start,0),2) AS pct_delta FROM e JOIN b USING(entity_slug) ORDER BY abs_delta DESC;
   Returned (top): codex +5,459 (6.02%); claude-code +4,720 (3.58%); n8n +3,392 (1.76%, base 192,197->195,589); vllm +2,960 (3.58%); langchain +2,125; supabase +1,791; crewai +1,768; ollama +1,735.

2. The star growth is perfectly monotonic — n8n gained stars on every single one of the 26 daily captures (~100–170/day), with zero flat or down days. Steady structural adoption, not a single spike jump.
   SQL: SELECT captured_at::date AS d, value FROM entity_stats WHERE entity_slug='n8n' AND metric='github_stars' ORDER BY captured_at;
   Returned: 192,197 (06-12) rising every capture to 195,589 (07-08); daily increments all positive, range ~+47 to +260, typical ~+120.

3. Docker is n8n's real distribution channel, and there it leads: +9,500,369 pulls in 27 days (~352k/day, monotonic) — the LARGEST absolute Docker-pull gain of any tracked application, and #2 by percentage (4.29%) behind only ollama (5.33%). The three bigger raw numbers (postgres/redis/mysql) are ambient CI base-images, not application adoption.
   SQL: WITH b AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value AS s FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='dockerhub_pulls_total' AND captured_at::date>='2026-06-12' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at ASC), e AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value AS v FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='dockerhub_pulls_total' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at DESC) SELECT e.entity_slug, (e.v-b.s) AS abs_delta, round(100.0*(e.v-b.s)/NULLIF(b.s,0),2) AS pct FROM e JOIN b USING(entity_slug) ORDER BY pct DESC;
   Returned: ollama +7,655,760 (5.33%); n8n +9,500,369 (4.29%); postgres +144.5M (1.34%); redis +117.8M (1.09%); mysql +31.9M (0.64%). n8n docker total 221,505,068->231,005,437.

4. npm downloads corroborate: +29.1% over the window (65,169->84,149/wk), the #2 growth rate of any npm package with a >20k weekly base, behind only astro (29.9%). Caveat: n8n's npm figure is a small, noisy fraction of its true install base (peaked ~92.5k on 06-28, dipped to ~78.7k early July) because most deployments are Docker/self-hosted, not `npm i` — which is why point 3 matters more.
   SQL: WITH b AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value AS s FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='npm_downloads_weekly' AND captured_at::date>='2026-06-12' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at ASC), e AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value AS v FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='npm_downloads_weekly' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at DESC) SELECT e.entity_slug, b.s, e.v, round(100.0*(e.v-b.s)/NULLIF(b.s,0),1) AS pct FROM e JOIN b USING(entity_slug) WHERE b.s>20000 ORDER BY pct DESC LIMIT 15;
   Returned (top): astro 29.9%; n8n 29.1%; supabase 25.3%; nextjs 23.8%; react-native 20.7%; typescript 16.4%.

5. THE QUIET PART — n8n's conversation share of voice on HN + Lobsters is negligible and flat: 0.14%–0.36% of tagged stories every month of 2026, no trend (Jan 0.29%, Feb 0.24%, Mar 0.36%, Apr 0.14%, May 0.24%, Jun 0.27%, Jul partial 0.15%). Raw counts 1–12 distinct stories/month. A tool deployed ~352k times a day generates ~7 conversations a month.
   SQL: WITH conv AS (SELECT url_hash, entities, source_created_at FROM raw_items WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at>='2026-01-01'), monthly AS (SELECT date_trunc('month',source_created_at)::date AS mo, count(DISTINCT url_hash) FILTER (WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'n8n')) AS n8n_stories, count(DISTINCT url_hash) FILTER (WHERE jsonb_array_length(entities)>0) AS tagged FROM conv GROUP BY 1) SELECT mo, n8n_stories, tagged, round(100.0*n8n_stories/NULLIF(tagged,0),2) AS sov FROM monthly ORDER BY mo;
   Returned: 2026-01 2/688/0.29; 02 7/2965/0.24; 03 12/3296/0.36; 04 4/2917/0.14; 05 6/2490/0.24; 06 7/2583/0.27; 07(partial) 1/648/0.15.

6. THE DIVERGENCE, quantified — the two repos immediately ABOVE n8n in star growth are conversation giants; n8n is a ghost. In June 2026, codex (+5,459 stars) held 5.15% SoV (133 stories) and claude-code (+4,720 stars) held 9.83% SoV (254 stories), vs n8n's 0.27% (7 stories). codex draws ~19x and claude-code ~36x n8n's conversation while adding a comparable number of stars.
   SQL: WITH conv AS (SELECT url_hash, jsonb_array_elements_text(entities) AS slug FROM raw_items WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at>='2026-06-01' AND source_created_at<'2026-07-01'), tot AS (SELECT count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS t FROM raw_items WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at>='2026-06-01' AND source_created_at<'2026-07-01' AND jsonb_array_length(entities)>0) SELECT slug, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS stories, round(100.0*count(DISTINCT url_hash)/(SELECT t FROM tot),2) AS sov FROM conv GROUP BY slug ORDER BY stories DESC LIMIT 15;
   Returned (top): claude 24.78%; anthropic 11.46%; rust 9.83%; claude-code 9.83%; openai 8.05%; mcp 7.67%; gpt 7.12%; python 5.57%; codex 5.15%; postgres 3.76%; cursor 3.21%; github-copilot 2.52%. (n8n not in top 15.)

7. Supporting builder signal: n8n's new_repos_weekly (new GitHub repos referencing it) ran 157 -> 129 -> 186 across three weekly captures — a live if noisy ecosystem building ON n8n. Only 3 data points; present as directional colour.
   SQL: SELECT captured_at::date d, value FROM entity_stats WHERE entity_slug='n8n' AND metric='new_repos_weekly' ORDER BY captured_at; -> 06-22:157, 06-29:129, 07-06:186.

WHY n8n SPECIFICALLY, NOT "AI AUTOMATION BROADLY": tested broadening to an agent-tooling cohort (crewai +3.31%, langchain +1.53%, llamaindex +1.24% all grew too). Recommend AGAINST broadening: (a) dilutes a clean single-repo story with a monotonic curve and a specific 195k-star identity; (b) langchain/crewai/llamaindex overlap published AI-coding coverage, whereas n8n is distinctly low-code/self-hosted workflow — a different, un-told character. Keep the agent era as CONTEXT, not the subject.
context: Adjacent to but distinct from published "The AI Coding Wars" (codex/claude-code appear only as conversation-heavy contrast cases just above n8n in star growth) and "MCP adoption rising while argument ends" (same adoption-without-argument shape — n8n is the extreme version). No open bet covers n8n; could seed one.

title: uv is eating Python packaging
thesis: Astral's uv has quietly scaled to the download volume of the dominant AI SDKs and roughly 3x Django's — while generating almost no sustained argument — making it the fastest-consolidating tool in Python packaging, even though the data can't (yet) declare the packaging wars formally "over."
period: entity_stats snapshot 2026-06-12 -> 2026-07-08 (~27 days; caveat: only ~4 weeks of history). Conversation/engagement 2026-01 -> 2026-06 (HN + lobsters; July partial, excluded).
findings:
1. GitHub stars are the clean monotonic signal: +895 stars (+1.04%) over the window, rising on essentially every daily capture with no reversals — 86,313 -> 87,208. The "boring, uncontroversial accretion" that anchors the quiet-winner read.
   SQL: SELECT metric, value, captured_at FROM entity_stats WHERE entity_slug='uv' AND metric IN ('pypi_downloads_weekly','github_stars') ORDER BY metric, captured_at
   Returned (stars, selected): 2026-06-12 86,313 -> 06-20 86,607 -> 06-30 86,909 -> 07-08 87,208. Every step positive.

2. STRESS-TEST FAILED on monotonicity of PyPI: uv's weekly PyPI downloads are NOT monotonic. They rose to a peak of 39,109,282 on 2026-06-21, then declined on eight consecutive captures down to 35,494,572 by 2026-07-05. The headline "+7.2% (33.1M -> 35.5M)" is an endpoint artifact hiding a peak-and-fade. Weekly figures are a noisy rolling 7-day sum (CI-driven), so describe as "flat-to-modestly-up, recently softening," NOT "keeps climbing."
   SQL: same query as Finding 1 (pypi_downloads_weekly rows).
   Returned (selected): 06-12 33.10M, 06-17 34.27M, 06-21 39.11M (peak), 06-23 38.54M, 06-26 37.36M, 06-30 36.85M, 07-03 36.31M, 07-05 35.49M.

3. Scale context — this is the real story: at ~35.5M weekly PyPI installs, uv is the largest pure developer tool tracked, sitting at AI-SDK scale. It runs neck-and-neck with the Anthropic SDK (37.1M) and is 3.2x Django's 11.0M. Everything ahead of it is an AI SDK/framework (openai 86.7M, langchain 74.2M, mcp 68.3M, gemini 68.1M), not a packaging/runtime tool. Caveat: uv's +7.2% is the slowest growth among the AI-adjacent leaders (mcp +36.5%, openai +22.5%, gemini +25.6%) — the story is scale + consolidation, not momentum.
   SQL: WITH latest AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='pypi_downloads_weekly' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at DESC), first AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='pypi_downloads_weekly' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at ASC) SELECT l.entity_slug, f.value first_val, l.value latest_val, round(100.0*(l.value-f.value)/NULLIF(f.value,0),1) pct_change FROM latest l JOIN first f USING(entity_slug) ORDER BY l.value DESC
   Returned (selected): openai 70.7M->86.7M (+22.5%); langchain ->74.2M (+15.1%); mcp ->68.3M (+36.5%); gemini ->68.1M (+25.6%); anthropic ->37.1M (+18.2%); uv 33.1M->35.5M (+7.2%); django 11.1M->11.0M (-0.8%); mistral -16.5%; vllm -14.2%.

4. Conversation is low-volume but high-engagement — the "boring consensus" signal. uv draws only 2–8 distinct HN/lobsters stories per month, yet when it surfaces it spikes hard: May 2026 hit peak_score 328 / 145 comments on just 2 stories (avg score 135); March hit 89 / 81 comments on 7 stories. Not debated constantly — occasionally celebrated, then dropped. (raw_items only returns uv-tagged conversation from 2026-01 onward.)
   SQL: SELECT to_char(date_trunc('month',source_created_at),'YYYY-MM') mo, count(DISTINCT url_hash) stories, max(peak_score) max_score, round(avg(peak_score)) avg_score, max(peak_comments) max_comments FROM raw_items WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'uv') AND source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2025-10-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
   Returned: Jan 2 stories (max 5); Feb 6 (max 3); Mar 7 (max 89, 81 comments); Apr 8 (max 39, 55 comments); May 2 (max 328, avg 135, 145 comments); Jun 7 (max 40, 17 comments).

5. uv is invisible plumbing in hiring: job_mentions_7d is 0 on five of six captures (one lone mention on 06-22). Supports "infrastructure nobody thinks about" framing — but sample is tiny, mention only as colour, never a headline number.
   SQL: SELECT value, captured_at::date FROM entity_stats WHERE entity_slug='uv' AND metric='job_mentions_7d' ORDER BY captured_at
   Returned: 0,0,0,1,0,0.

6. DATA GAP (must caveat): no competing packaging tools are tracked — poetry, pip, rye, pdm, pipenv, hatch, conda, ruff, pixi all return zero rows in entity_stats. The dataset CANNOT show rivals declining, so the "packaging wars are over / uv won" claim cannot be evidenced against competitors here. Keep the claim to what the data supports: uv's own scale and quiet accretion.
   SQL: SELECT DISTINCT entity_slug FROM entity_stats WHERE entity_slug IN ('poetry','pip','rye','pdm','pipenv','hatch','conda','ruff','pixi') ORDER BY 1
   Returned: [] (empty).
context: Distinct from the four published stories — a runtime/tooling emergence story, not AI-coding, mobile, supply-chain, or MCP. Adjacent to the MCP open bets only as a methodological cousin (low-conversation + high-adoption "quiet winner" pattern); don't grade those bets here. The MCP piece is the closest narrative sibling — uv is the same shape in a different ecosystem.
candidate_bets:
- uv GitHub stars cross 90,000 by 2026-09-30. Why: monotonic +895/27d (~34/day) implies ~90.0–90.5k by end-Sept on a straight-line hold. Confidence medium-high (stars almost never reverse). 2026-09-30.
- uv weekly PyPI downloads stay above Django's by a 3x+ margin through 2026-09-30 (uv >= 3x django's weekly). Why: uv 35.5M vs django 11.0M today (3.2x); django flat, uv would need to collapse to lose the margin. Confidence high. 2026-09-30.
cover_idea: A single, unremarkable steel nut being tightened onto a bolt at the base of a towering machine — the machine (the Python/AI stack) draws all the eye, but the whole thing rests on this one quiet fastener. Muted palette, the nut in sharp focus, everything above it soft. "The part nobody's arguing about anymore."

the machine dominates and draws the eye upward while the small fastener at its foot sits in sharp focus, everything above it falling soft — the one quiet part on which the whole structure rests.","cover_alt":"A small steel nut on a bolt at the base of a large, elaborate machine, the nut in sharp focus and the machine above it soft, suggesting a modest part that everything else depends on.","brief":["uv's weekly PyPI installs sit at roughly 35.5 million, placing it at the scale of the major AI SDKs and about 3.2 times Django's 11.0 million.","GitHub stars are the clean signal: uv gained 895 stars (+1.04%) across the window, rising on essentially every daily capture with no reversals.","PyPI volume is not a story of relentless climb — it peaked at 39.1 million on 21 June and softened over eight consecutive captures to 35.5 million by 5 July, so the honest reading is flat-to-modestly-up and recently easing.","Conversation is low-volume but high-engagement: uv surfaces in only two to eight forum stories a month, yet a single May story reached a peak score of 328 and 145 comments.","uv is near-invisible in hiring signals, registering job mentions on just one of six captures — consistent with plumbing nobody thinks to name."],"sections":[{"type":"prose","heading":"A tool the size of an AI SDK","body":["The most-installed packages tracked in our dataset are, almost without exception, artefacts of the artificial-intelligence build-out: the OpenAI SDK at 86.7 million weekly PyPI installs, LangChain at 74.2 million, the Model Context Protocol libraries at 68.3 million, Google's Gemini SDK at 68.1 million. Amid that company sits an anomaly. uv — Astral's Rust-written Python package and project manager — records roughly 35.5 million weekly installs, running neck-and-neck with the Anthropic SDK's 37.1 million and standing as the largest pure developer tool we track. Everything installed more often than uv is an AI SDK or framework; nothing ahead of it is a packaging or runtime tool.","That is the finding worth sitting with. A piece of build-time plumbing has reached the download scale of the SDKs powering the current platform cycle, and it has done so without becoming a subject of sustained debate. The comparison that anchors it is domestic rather than exotic: at 35.5 million weekly installs, uv moves about 3.2 times the volume of Django, the framework that has defined server-side Python for a generation and which now sits at 11.0 million."]},{"type":"stats","heading":"Where uv sits","items":[{"label":"uv weekly PyPI installs","value":"35.5M","sub":"Largest pure developer tool tracked; +7.2% over the window"},{"label":"Versus Django","value":"~3.2x","sub":"uv 35.5M against Django's 11.0M (Django -0.8%)"},{"label":"Versus Anthropic SDK","value":"35.5M vs 37.1M","sub":"Neck-and-neck; the SDK grew +18.2%"},{"label":"GitHub stars","value":"+895","sub":"86,313 to 87,208 (+1.04%), rising on essentially every capture"},{"label":"Job mentions (7-day)","value":"1 of 6 captures","sub":"Zero on five captures; a lone mention on 22 June"}]},{"type":"prose","heading":"Scale, not momentum","body":["It would be easy to mistake this for a growth story, and the data does not support that mistake. Weekly PyPI downloads are a noisy rolling seven-day sum, driven heavily by continuous-integration traffic, and uv's figure did not climb steadily. It rose to a peak of 39.1 million on 21 June, then declined on eight consecutive captures to 35.5 million by 5 July. The headline delta of +7.2% — from 33.1 million to 35.5 million across the window — is an endpoint artefact that conceals a peak-and-fade. The right description is flat-to-modestly-up and recently softening, not relentless ascent.","The point is confirmed by the company uv keeps. Among the AI-adjacent leaders, uv posted the slowest growth of the group: the MCP libraries rose 36.5%, the Gemini SDK 25.6%, the OpenAI SDK 22.5%, over the same window. uv's +7.2% is the profile of a tool that has already arrived, not one still climbing. The story here is consolidation at scale, not velocity.","GitHub stars are the one clean, monotonic signal in the picture. uv added 895 stars — 86,313 to 87,208, a rise of 1.04% — and did so on essentially every daily capture with no reversals. Stars are a lagging, low-noise proxy for goodwill rather than usage, and their steadiness is the boring, uncontroversial accretion that marks a tool settling into the furniture."]},{"type":"table","heading":"The most-installed Python packages, and where a tool sits among them","columns":["Package","First","Latest","Change"],"rows":[["openai","70.7M","86.7M","+22.5%"],["langchain","—","74.2M","+15.1%"],["mcp","—","68.3M","+36.5%"],["gemini","—","68.1M","+25.6%"],["anthropic","—","37.1M","+18.2%"],["uv","33.1M","35.5M","+7.2%"],["django","11.1M","11.0M","-0.8%"]]},{"type":"prose","heading":"The silence is the signal","body":["What distinguishes uv is not how much it is discussed but how little. Across Hacker News and Lobsters it surfaces in only two to eight distinct stories a month. Yet when it does surface, it spikes: a single May story reached a peak score of 328 with 145 comments on just two stories that month, an average score of 135; March produced a story scoring 89 with 81 comments. This is not a tool argued over daily. It is occasionally celebrated, then set down — the conversational fingerprint of consensus rather than contest.","The hiring signal points the same way. uv registered a non-zero seven-day job-mention count on only one of six captures, a lone mention on 22 June and zero otherwise. That sample is far too small to carry a number, and we offer it only as colour: uv appears to be the kind of infrastructure nobody thinks to name in a job posting, present by assumption rather than by request.","A caution on what this dataset can and cannot show. It tracks none of uv's rivals — pip, Poetry, PDM, Rye, Pipenv, Hatch, conda, pixi and ruff all return no rows — so we cannot demonstrate any competitor declining, and we make no claim that a 'packaging war' has been formally won. What the data supports is narrower and firmer: uv's own scale, and its quiet accretion, are those of a tool that has become load-bearing.","Two further caveats bound the reading. The adoption figures are a snapshot, not a trend: entity_stats holds only about four weeks of history, from 12 June to 8 July 2026, so short-run moves such as the late-June PyPI fade should not be over-read. And the conversation sample is skewed toward Hacker News and Lobsters, communities that over-index on early-adopter tooling; July 2026 is partial and is excluded from the monthly conversation counts."]}],"methodology":"Adoption figures are drawn from the entity_stats table over a roughly four-week snapshot, 12 June to 8 July 2026 (~27 days), covering the pypi_downloads_weekly, github_stars and job_mentions_7d metrics; percentage changes are computed as the delta between the first and last capture in the window. Cross-tool ranking uses DISTINCT ON to take each entity's first and latest weekly-download capture. Conversation figures come from raw_items filtered to Hacker News and Lobsters sources with uv-tagged entities, aggregated by month from October 2025; uv-tagged conversation is only present from January 2026 onward. Caveats stated plainly: the adoption window is a ~4-week snapshot rather than a trend, weekly PyPI downloads are a noisy CI-driven rolling seven-day sum (so uv's +7.2% is an endpoint artefact over a peak-and-fade), the conversation sample skews to early-adopter forums, the job-mention sample is too small to anchor a claim, July 2026 is partial and excluded from monthly counts, and no competing packaging tools are tracked so no claim is made against rivals.","evidence":[{"query":"SELECT metric, value, captured_at FROM entity_stats WHERE entity_slug='uv' AND metric IN ('pypi_downloads_weekly','github_stars') ORDER BY metric, captured_at","finding":"GitHub stars (selected): 2026-06-12 86,313 -> 06-20 86,607 -> 06-30 86,909 -> 07-08 87,208; every step positive, +895 (+1.04%)."},{"query":"SELECT metric, value, captured_at FROM entity_stats WHERE entity_slug='uv' AND metric IN ('pypi_downloads_weekly','github_stars') ORDER BY metric, captured_at","finding":"pypi_downloads_weekly (selected): 06-12 33.10M, 06-17 34.27M, 06-21 39.11M (peak), 06-23 38.54M, 06-26 37.36M, 06-30 36.85M, 07-03 36.31M, 07-05 35.49M — not monotonic; rose to a peak then declined on eight consecutive captures."},{"query":"WITH latest AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='pypi_downloads_weekly' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at DESC), first AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='pypi_downloads_weekly' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at ASC) SELECT l.entity_slug, f.value first_val, l.value latest_val, round(100.0*(l.value-f.value)/NULLIF(f.value,0),1) pct_change FROM latest l JOIN first f USING(entity_slug) ORDER BY l.value DESC","finding":"Selected: openai 70.7M->86.7M (+22.5%); everything above it is an AI SDK/framework."},{"query":"SELECT to_char(date_trunc('month',source_created_at),'YYYY-MM') mo, count(DISTINCT url_hash) stories, max(peak_score) max_score, round(avg(peak_score)) avg_score, max(peak_comments) max_comments FROM raw_items WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'uv') AND source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2025-10-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1","finding":"Jan 2 stories (max 5); No competing packaging tools are tracked, so the dataset cannot evidence any rival's decline."}],"bets":[{"claim":"uv's GitHub stars cross 90,000 by 30 September 2026.","rationale":"Monotonic gain of 895 stars over 27 days (~34/day) with no reversals implies roughly 90.0–90.5k by end-September on a straight-line hold, and stars almost never reverse.","confidence":"medium","horizon":"2026-09-30"},{"claim":"uv's weekly PyPI downloads stay above Django's by a 3x-or-greater margin through 30 September 2026.","rationale":"uv is at 35.5M against Django's 11.0M today (3.2x); Django is flat, so uv would need to collapse to lose the margin.","confidence":"high","horizon":"2026-09-30"}]}

title: Kubernetes/Docker — quiet in the feed, top of the payroll
thesis: Kubernetes and Docker have gone quiet in developer conversation while quietly sitting at the very top of what employers actually hire for — the mirror image of the AI tools that dominate the discourse but barely register in job ads: the argument-vs-paycheck gap has split wide open around "boring" container infrastructure.
period: Conversation trend Feb–Jun 2026 (July partial, flagged). Hiring snapshot: 4-week window 2026-06-12 -> 2026-07-06, latest capture 2026-07-06.
findings:
1. Container-infra conversation is in clear multi-month decline on HN + Lobsters. Kubernetes distinct stories fell from a March peak of 72 to 29 in June (-60%; -44% vs Feb's 52); Docker fell from 60 in March to 29 in June (-52%; -43% vs Feb's 51). July is on a similar low pace (k8s 20, docker 13 in 8 days).
   SQL: SELECT date_trunc('month', source_created_at)::date AS mo, count(DISTINCT url_hash) FILTER (WHERE jsonb_array_length(entities) > 0) AS tagged_total, count(DISTINCT url_hash) FILTER (WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'kubernetes')) AS k8s, count(DISTINCT url_hash) FILTER (WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'docker')) AS docker FROM raw_items WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-02-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-09' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
   Returned: Feb tagged=2965 k8s=52 docker=51; Mar 3296/72/60; Apr 2917/44/32; May 2490/53/34; Jun 2583/29/29; Jul(partial) 648/20/13.

2. As share of voice (% of that month's tagged mentions) the decline holds Feb->Jun: k8s 1.75% (52/2965) -> 1.12% (29/2583), -36%; docker 1.72% -> 1.12%, -35%. From the March peak steeper: k8s 2.18% (72/3296) -> 1.12%, -49%; docker 1.82% -> 1.12%, -38%. HONEST CAVEAT: July SoV bounces up (k8s 3.09%=20/648, docker 2.01%=13/648) because the partial month's denominator is tiny (648 tagged stories in 8 days) — do NOT lead with July SoV; the defensible claim is the Feb–Jun / March-peak decline on both raw counts and SoV. (Same SQL/result as Finding 1.)

3. Kubernetes ranks #2 of all 65 tracked entities in hiring, behind only the Python language; it is the #1 platform/infrastructure tool by a wide margin. Docker sits in the top tier (#8, tied). Job mentions: python 30, kubernetes 10, typescript 7, react 7, golang 6, claude 5, github-copilot 5, docker 4, vue 4.
   SQL: WITH latest AS (SELECT DISTINCT ON (entity_slug) entity_slug, value, captured_at FROM entity_stats WHERE metric='job_mentions_7d' ORDER BY entity_slug, captured_at DESC) SELECT entity_slug, value, captured_at::date FROM latest ORDER BY value DESC
   Returned (top): python 30, kubernetes 10, typescript 7, react 7, golang 6, claude 5, github-copilot 5, docker 4, vue 4, react-native/postgres/php/django/flutter 3.

4. The divergence is a true mirror image — the tools that OWN the conversation are nearly absent from hiring, and vice versa. June conversation top entities: claude (640 stories), anthropic (296), rust (254), claude-code (254), openai (208), mcp (198), gpt (184), codex (133), cursor (83). Yet in the hiring snapshot codex=0, cursor=1, claude-code=1, claude=5. Kubernetes (29 June stories, ~22x quieter than claude) out-hires every one of them. AI-infrastructure tools are hiring-invisible: ollama 0, vllm 0, langchain 1, llamaindex 0, crewai 0.
   SQL (conversation): WITH e AS (SELECT DISTINCT url_hash, jsonb_array_elements_text(entities) AS slug FROM raw_items WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-06-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-01') SELECT slug, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS stories FROM e GROUP BY slug ORDER BY stories DESC LIMIT 15
   Returned: claude 640, anthropic 296, rust 254, claude-code 254, openai 208, mcp 198, gpt 184, python 144, codex 133, postgres 97, cursor 83, github-copilot 65, cloudflare 57, gemini 50, deepseek 47. (Hiring numbers from Finding 3.)

5. Aggregate framing stat: Docker + Kubernetes hiring mentions combined (14) roughly equal ALL AI-coding tools combined (claude 5 + copilot 5 + cursor 1 + claude-code 1 + aider 1 + codex/windsurf/cline/zed 0 = 13) and dwarf all AI-infra tools combined (langchain 1 + n8n 1 + ollama/vllm/llamaindex/crewai 0 = 2). Two "boring," decade-old container tools carry as much hiring weight as the entire loud AI-coding category. (Derived by summing Finding 3's full ranking.)

MANDATORY CAVEAT: job_mentions_7d is a small UK-skewed job-feed rolling count with only ~4-week history (2026-06-12 -> 2026-07-08); absolute values tiny (top entity = 30) and week-to-week noisy — kubernetes ran 2->3->4->10->10 over the window. Treat as directional rank evidence ("k8s/docker at the top of hiring"), NOT precise volume. Conversation data (HN + Lobsters, ~1.1M items) is far more robust.
context: Deliberately the inverse of published "AI Coding Wars" — that piece raced the AI tools against each other; this one uses their hiring-invisibility only as the contrast that makes container-infra hiring strength legible. Adjacent to "MCP argument is ending, adoption is not" — same shape (discourse cooling != real-world usage cooling) applied to infrastructure. No open bet covers containers or hiring — greenfield.
candidate_bets:
- Kubernetes will remain in the top 3 entities by job_mentions_7d in the next capture after 2026-09-30, despite staying outside the top 10 entities by monthly conversation share of voice. Why: hiring rank stable/rising (2->10) while conversation SoV falls. Confidence medium (hiring sample small). 2026-09-30.
- Combined Kubernetes + Docker conversation SoV on HN/Lobsters stays at or below its Feb 2026 level (~3.5% combined) through Q3. Why: clean six-month downtrend, no catalyst. Confidence medium-high. 2026-09-30.
cover_idea: A split-frame diptych. Left: a loud, crowded neon-lit conference stage / social feed packed with AI-tool logos and chat bubbles — all noise, no substance. Right: a quiet, dim shipping-container yard at night, two weathered containers (subtly a Kubernetes helm and a Docker whale silhouette) stacked and padlocked, a lone forklift with its lights on — empty of crowds but clearly where the work (and money) moves. The party is in one yard; the payroll is in the other.

title: Bun — anatomy of a runtime hype cycle
thesis: Bun's spring-2026 conversation spike was a controversy — a wave of doubt over its Zig-to-Rust rewrite, not celebration — that peaked on HN in May and collapsed by summer, yet its actual install base kept climbing right through and after the noise; a textbook case of adoption decoupling from buzz, where even negative hype had no measurable effect on real usage.
period: Conversation Feb-Jun 2026 (Jan/Jul shown as context; Jul partial). Adoption snapshot Jun 12 - Jul 8 2026 (the only window entity_stats covers — a hard caveat, see Finding 3).
findings:
1. The conversation spike is real and sharp — bun HN/lobsters stories tripled into a May peak, then collapsed. Distinct stories/month: Feb 9, Mar 13, Apr 18, May 43, Jun 18, Jul 1 (partial). May is a clean ~2.4x jump over April and ~5x over January (3).
   SQL: SELECT to_char(date_trunc('month', source_created_at),'YYYY-MM') AS mo, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS stories FROM raw_items WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'bun') AND source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-01-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
   Returned: 2026-01->3, 02->9, 03->13, 04->18, 05->43, 06->18, 07->1.

2. The spike was driven entirely by the Zig->Rust rewrite saga — and the tone was anxiety/controversy, not hype. Highest-engagement May stories by HN peak_score: "Bun's experimental Rust rewrite hits 99.8% test compatibility" (709 pts, 690 comments), "Bun ported to Rust in 6 days" (698), "Rewrite Bun in Rust has been merged" (689, 769 comments), "Bun support is now limited and deprecated" [yt-dlp dropping Bun] (573), "I am worried about Bun" (516), "Bun Rust rewrite: codebase fails basic miri checks, allows for UB in safe rust" (480), "Electrobun 2.0 will be decoupled from Bun due to the Rust rewrite" (148). Major front-page events (peak_score 480-709, 340-769 comments each) — the spike is NOT thin: a high-engagement, doubt-laden pile-on about rewrite risk and downstream projects dropping Bun.
   SQL: SELECT title, peak_score, peak_comments, to_char(source_created_at,'YYYY-MM-DD') AS posted FROM raw_items WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'bun') AND source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-04-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-06-01' ORDER BY peak_score DESC NULLS LAST LIMIT 20
   Returned: titles/scores above.

3. Adoption kept climbing while the buzz was already gone. In the Jun 12 - Jul 8 snapshot — the weeks after the May conversation peak, when story volume had already fallen from 43 to 18 — npm weekly downloads rose from 2,163,164 to 2,417,449 (+11.8%, peaking at 2,698,341 around Jun 30-Jul 3) and GitHub stars rose cleanly/monotonically from 93,123 to 93,636 (+513, +0.55%). Honest caveats: (a) the download series is noisy, not monotonic day-to-day — climbs to ~2.70M then the partial-July tail dips to 2.42M (likely npm's lagging partial-week reporting; weekend-dip pattern visible throughout); (b) entity_stats history only starts Jun 12, so it post-dates the May spike — cannot show downloads rising DURING the controversy, only that they kept rising in its aftermath. Stars are the cleaner monotonic signal.
   SQL: SELECT to_char(captured_at,'YYYY-MM-DD') AS d, metric, value FROM entity_stats WHERE entity_slug='bun' AND metric IN ('npm_downloads_weekly','github_stars') ORDER BY metric, captured_at
   Returned: stars 93123->93636 (monotone); npm 2,163,164 (Jun12) -> 2,698,341 (Jun30) -> 2,417,449 (Jul8).

4. The spike was Bun-specific, not a runtime-wide moment — deno stayed flat, node steady. Distinct stories/month — bun: 9/13/18/43/18; deno: 5/11/0/5/7; node(+nodejs): 24/30/15/17/15 (Feb-Jun). Deno never spiked, confirming this was Bun's own event, not category noise.
   SQL: SELECT to_char(date_trunc('month',source_created_at),'YYYY-MM') AS mo, count(DISTINCT url_hash) FILTER (WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'bun')) AS bun, count(DISTINCT url_hash) FILTER (WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'deno')) AS deno, count(DISTINCT url_hash) FILTER (WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'node') OR jsonb_exists(entities,'nodejs')) AS node FROM raw_items WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND source_created_at >= '2026-02-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-01' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
   Returned: as above.

5. Bun's share of voice within the runtime category doubled at the peak, then receded. Bun as % of all bun/deno/node runtime conversation: Feb 26.5%, Mar 26.5%, Apr 56.3%, May 66.2%, Jun 46.2%. Bun went from a quarter of runtime chatter to two-thirds at peak, then fell back — the buzz curve in one number.
   SQL: WITH runtime AS (SELECT date_trunc('month',source_created_at) m, count(DISTINCT url_hash) FILTER (WHERE jsonb_exists(entities,'bun')) bun, count(DISTINCT url_hash) AS total FROM raw_items WHERE source_slug IN ('hn_top','hn_new','hn_best','hn_show','hn_ask','lobsters') AND (jsonb_exists(entities,'bun') OR jsonb_exists(entities,'deno') OR jsonb_exists(entities,'node') OR jsonb_exists(entities,'nodejs')) AND source_created_at >= '2026-02-01' AND source_created_at < '2026-07-01' GROUP BY 1) SELECT to_char(m,'YYYY-MM') mo, bun, total, round(100.0*bun/total,1) AS bun_share_of_runtime FROM runtime ORDER BY 1
   Returned: Feb 26.5%, Mar 26.5%, Apr 56.3%, May 66.2%, Jun 46.2%.
context: Sibling of published "The Argument About MCP Is Ending; Its Adoption Is Not" (conversation cooling / adoption rising) — same shape, different subject. The mechanism here is distinctive: the buzz was negative (rewrite anxiety, deprecations), not enthusiasm, which makes the adoption-immunity finding sharper. No open bet covers runtime/bun; the MCP bets are analogous framing only — don't grade.
candidate_bets:
- Bun's runtime share-of-voice stays below its May 2026 peak (66%) through Q3. Why: controversy-driven spikes don't repeat; conversation already halved to 46% by June. Confidence medium. 2026-09-30.
- Bun npm weekly downloads exceed 2.6M again by 2026-09-30 (adoption resumes its climb past the July partial-week dip). Why: trailing dip is reporting lag; 27-day trend and monotone star growth point up. Confidence medium-low (short, noisy history). 2026-09-30.
cover_idea: A rocket's vapor trail (the loud, showy exhaust) dissipating into nothing against the sky — while below, unglamorous and ignored, a steadily rising column of water fills a reservoir behind a dam, tick marks climbing the wall. Loud thing fades up top; quiet thing keeps rising below. Alt: a stock-ticker crowd screaming and dispersing on a trading floor while, unnoticed in the corner, a simple line on a monitor keeps stepping upward.
